result:
Here is what comes, unedited, in the quiet of an early morning.

***

The light is just beginning to change. It’s not yet dawn, but the absolute black has softened to a deep, velvety blue. In this hour, the world feels like a held breath. My own breath is the loudest thing in the room, a steady rhythm against the silence. This is the time before the world remembers itself—before the birds begin their rehearsals, before the distant hum of the first car on the wet road, before the mind starts its relentless catalogue of tasks and anxieties.

I think about the word “liminal.” It’s become fashionable, but it’s accurate. This is a threshold time. I am neither of the night nor of the day. I am a creature in a doorway, and there is a peculiar freedom in that. In the full light of day, we are who we are: our jobs, our roles, our responsibilities. In the deep night, we are sleepers, adrift in private, unremembered worlds. But here, now, I am just a consciousness in a chair, watching the blue deepen.

My mind drifts to a memory, sharp as a splinter. I am eight years old, standing at the edge of a lake in October. The water is cold, clear, and still. I am throwing flat stones, trying to make them skip. One, two, three jumps, then a plunk. The concentric rings expand, perfect, overlapping, until they reach the shore and disappear. I remember the feeling wasn’t of triumph at a good skip, but of awe at the rings themselves. They were evidence. I had touched the surface of something vast, and it had responded with perfect, beautiful geometry. Then the water smoothed over, as if it had never happened.

Isn’t that most of life? We throw our stones—our efforts, our words, our loves—and for a moment we see the ripples. They seem significant, they interact with other ripples from other stones. But eventually, the lake forgets. The surface becomes placid again. The stone is resting somewhere in the murky dark, unseen. We are left with the memory of the ripples, the feeling of the throw in our arm.

The blue outside is now tinged with a thin line of sulphur-yellow on the horizon. The first bird, a robin I think, tentatively offers a few notes. Not yet a song, just a clearing of the throat. It is answered by another, farther away. The conversation begins.

I think about the people still asleep in the houses I can see. The young parents exhausted, clutching an hour more of silence. The old man whose sleep is thin and fractured. The student dreaming of an exam they forgot to study for. We are all islands in the night, but the dawn is a tide that connects us again, slowly covering the lonely sandbars between us. Soon, we will all be back in the continent of day, pretending we were never separate at all.

There’s a weight to being the only one awake. You feel like a witness. You are keeping vigil for the sleeping world. It’s a gentle responsibility. You must be quiet. You must pay attention. You must remember this moment of peace so you can report back, in some indirect way, through a kinder word or a more patient sigh, that the world is still beautiful and quiet at its core, before it puts on its noisy, complicated costume.

I think about the internet, that other world that never sleeps. Its frantic, scrolling eye is closed to me now. This is the real world, the one of shifting light and cooling coffee. This is the world that was here long before servers and algorithms, and will be here long after. The internet feels like a city—all light and noise and crowd. This early morning is the ancient forest outside its walls. This is where you come to remember you have a body that breathes, eyes that adjust to gradations of dark, and a mind that can wander without a map or a destination.

My coffee is gone. The yellow line has bled upwards, diluting the blue into a pale, watery grey. Shapes in the garden define themselves: the skeletal outline of the birch tree, the dark mound of the rosemary bush. The world is drawing itself in charcoal, waiting for the color to be added later.

I think of all the mornings I have missed. All the times I have slapped a snooze button and rejected this gift of quiet witness. We treat time as a currency to be spent, but in hours like this, you understand it is more like a landscape to be inhabited. You are not spending it; you are standing in it. You are letting it flow around you, and for once, you are not trying to dam it or divert it or bottle it.

The first car hisses by on the damp street. A light flicks on in a house across the way. The spell is breaking. The threshold is being crossed. I feel a faint pang of loss, the same feeling as watching the last ember of a campfire fade to grey ash. But I am not sad. The transition is the point. You cannot appreciate the day without knowing the night. You cannot value connection without having known solitude.

I will move soon. I will shower, dress, speak, work, engage. I will become a person in the world again, with all its complexities and collisions. But I will carry this hour inside me like a secret locket. Inside it: the blue light, the memory of the cold lake, the taste of bitter coffee, the silent vigil for a sleeping planet.

The day is here now. Full, plain, undramatic light fills the room. The birds are in full, argumentative chorus. The world has remembered itself. It has its lists, its appointments, its urgencies. And I have mine.

But for a thousand words, or for a single, stretched-out hour, I existed in the doorway. I was the watcher in the blue, the keeper of the quiet, the one who saw the world not as a problem to be solved, but as a mystery to be observed, just for a little while, before joining it once more.
